name: Aldo Leopold Bench
notes: Made for my sister's wedding. The design comes from Aldo Leopold himself — he built the original from Wisconsin River materials. The trick is you sit backward, facing away, so you can use binoculars or a camera without leaning forward. It's a bench designed for watching.

name: Fallen Ash High Table
notes: The wood came from trees that fell in storms and was given away as slabs at the 2014 Atwood Festival in Madison. I dried it in my garage for two years before building. The hairpin legs came off a tiled table I found on the street. It ended up taller than I intended — works well as a TV stand.

name: The City and Coming Climate — Figures
notes: Led graphics production for Brian Stone's book published by Cambridge University Press. Produced and reproduced all scientific figures — maps, charts, and diagrams — translating climate research into visual arguments. Working at that scale with a serious academic audience taught me that design isn't decoration; it's how ideas reach people.

name: Madison B-Cycle T-Shirt
notes: Competition entry for Madison's bike share launch. Abstracted the shapes of Mendota and Monona into a single graphic, then overlaid the station network — names tilted at 45° in the style of a subway map. Tried to make the case that B-Cycle belonged in the same conversation as transit, not just leisure.

name: The Greenway Effect
notes: My kid posted a QR code flier on the Albany Greenway asking cyclists to rate their mood before, during, and after biking on a 1–5 scale. 51 riders responded over 16 collection days. Average mood before: 3.1. After: 4.4. Not one rider finished below a 4. The responses became a data art poster — a spoke wheel where each spoke is a rider, with rings marking mood scores and alternating day bands as a bezel. A science project and a community one.

name: i(V)Pod
notes: Personal project riffing on Apple's iconic silhouette ad campaign. The iPod was everywhere that year — it felt like the first time a piece of consumer technology had a genuine visual identity, not just a product. This was a response to that moment, and probably the start of paying serious attention to design as communication.
